key_insight: The paper proposes a self-balancing sequential sampler that adaptively biases choices to make empirical frequencies converge to a target distribution at the fastest possible rate while preserving unpredictability.
engineering_relevance: This is useful for scheduling, auditing, representative sampling, and treatment assignment where you want fewer repeats and long gaps without making future selections easy to exploit.
method: Adaptive sampling probabilities are updated sequentially to counteract imbalance in the current empirical counts.
The sampler is characterized as a Markovian rule with an invariance property and as a stochastic mirror-descent method.
An entropy-regularized optimization view identifies the sampler as the unique solution balancing convergence speed and unpredictability.
evidence_signal: The abstract claims the method achieves an explicit O(n^-1) convergence rate, beats the IID O(n^-1/2) baseline, and has a diffusive Ornstein-Uhlenbeck limit in the weak-bias regime.
watch_item: Performance depends on the chosen biasing strength, and overly aggressive biasing may reduce unpredictability or make implementation sensitive to parameter tuning.
paper_title: Self-Balancing Sequential Sampling: Fast Convergence with Controlled Predictability
